title: “Monks, the defilements of gold — because of which gold becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly workable — likewise,…
content: “Monks, the defilements of gold — because of which gold becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly workable — likewise, monks, there are these five defilements of the mind because of which the mind becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly concentrated for the destruction of the defilements. What five? Sensual desire, monks, is a defilement of the mind. Because of this defilement, the mind becomes defiled, not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly concentrated for the destruction of the defilements. Ill-will … sloth and torpor … restlessness and remorse … doubt are defilements of the mind. These, monks, are the five defilements of the mind. Because of these defilements, the mind becomes not malleable, not wieldy, not radiant, brittle, and not properly concentrated for the destruction of the defilements.”

title: “Householders, if those wanderers of other sects ask you: ‘By what basis, by what tradition, do you speak of those venerable ones as follows: Surely…
content: “Householders, if those wanderers of other sects ask you: ‘By what basis, by what tradition, do you speak of those venerable ones as follows: Surely those venerable ones are free from lust, or are on the path to subduing lust; free from ill-will, or on the path to subduing ill-will; free from delusion, or on the path to subduing delusion?’—when asked thus, householders, you should answer those wanderers of other sects: ‘Those venerable ones dwell in remote lodgings in isolated forests. But in such places there are no forms cognizable by the eye that they could see and then delight in; no sounds cognizable by the ear … no odors cognizable by the nose … no tastes cognizable by the tongue … no tangibles cognizable by the body that they could feel and then delight in. Good sirs, by this basis, by this tradition, we speak of those venerable ones as follows: Surely those venerable ones are free from lust, or are on the path to subduing lust; free from ill-will … free from delusion, or on the path to subduing delusion.’”

title: “Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple dwells with aroused energy, abandoning unwholesome states and acquiring wholesome states, steadfast, vigorous, not shirking the burden of wholesome states.…
content: “Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple dwells with aroused energy, abandoning unwholesome states and acquiring wholesome states, steadfast, vigorous, not shirking the burden of wholesome states. He has mindfulness, endowed with supreme mindfulness and discernment, recollecting and recalling what was done and said long ago. He has wisdom, endowed with wisdom regarding arising and passing away, noble and penetrating, leading to the complete destruction of suffering. Thus, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is endowed with noble qualities.”

title: “I do not see any other contact, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a man as the contact of a woman. The…
content: “I do not see any other contact, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a man as the contact of a woman. The contact of a woman, monks, obsesses and occupies the mind of a man.”

title: Speak with a person Who holds a sword in hand, Speak with a demon, Or sit closely with them. A poisonous snake — If bitten,…
content:
Speak with a person
Who holds a sword in hand,
Speak with a demon,
Or sit closely with them.
A poisonous snake —
If bitten, one dies —
But do not alone
Speak with a woman.
Mindless, they bind one
With glances, with smiles,
With revealing clothes,
With sweet words —
Still not satisfied,
One becomes unconscious and dies.
These five strands of sensual pleasure
Are seen in female form —
Form, sound, smell, taste, touch —
Enticing and agreeable.
Overwhelmed by the waterfall of sensuality,
Flooded and swept away,
The foolish and ignorant,
Not understanding sensual pleasures.
Humans, at death,
Go to states of existence and non-existence —
They are led on
Through many births.
Those who understand sensual pleasures,
Without fear in their conduct,
Reach the far shore,
Attaining the destruction of the defilements.

title: “Monks, suppose a person is being swept away by a river that is lovely and pleasant. A man with good eyesight, standing on the bank,…
content: “Monks, suppose a person is being swept away by a river that is lovely and pleasant. A man with good eyesight, standing on the bank, seeing him, says: ‘Good man, though you are being swept away by this lovely, pleasant river, further down there is a lake with waves and whirlpools, with crocodiles and demons. If you reach that place, you will die or suffer deadly pain.’ Then that person, hearing his words, struggles upstream with his hands and feet. Monks, I have given this simile to make the meaning clear. The meaning here is this: ‘The river,’ monks, is a synonym for craving. ‘Lovely and pleasant’ is a synonym for the six internal sense bases. ‘The lake further down’ is a synonym for the five lower fetters. ‘Waves’ is a synonym for anger and distress. ‘Whirlpools’ is a synonym for the five strands of sensual pleasure. ‘Crocodiles and demons’ is a synonym for women. ‘Struggling upstream’ is a synonym for renunciation. ‘Struggling with hands and feet’ is a synonym for arousing energy. ‘The man with good eyesight standing on the bank’ is a synonym for the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One.”

title: “What is the cause, good sirs, what is the condition for the arising of unarisen greed, and for the growth and increase of arisen greed?”…
content: “What is the cause, good sirs, what is the condition for the arising of unarisen greed, and for the growth and increase of arisen greed?” - “The sign of the beautiful.” For one who attends unwisely to the sign of the beautiful, unarisen greed arises and arisen greed increases and grows. This, good sirs, is the cause, this is the condition for the arising of unarisen greed and for the growth and increase of arisen greed.

title: “Sāriputta, while a noble disciple attains and dwells in the rapture born of seclusion, during that time five things do not occur for him: pain…
content: “Sāriputta, while a noble disciple attains and dwells in the rapture born of seclusion, during that time five things do not occur for him: pain and grief connected with sensuality do not occur; pleasure and joy connected with sensuality do not occur; pain and grief connected with the unwholesome do not occur; pleasure and joy connected with the unwholesome do not occur; pain and grief connected with the wholesome do not occur. Sāriputta, while a noble disciple attains and dwells in the rapture born of seclusion, during that time these five things do not occur for him.”

title: “Monks, whatever is not yours, abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being. And what, monks, is not yours? The eye, monks,…
content: “Monks, whatever is not yours, abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being. And what, monks, is not yours? The eye, monks, is not yours — abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being. Forms are not yours — abandon them. Eye-consciousness is not yours — abandon it. Eye-contact is not yours — abandon it. Whatever feeling arises dependent on eye-contact — pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant — that feeling is not yours. Abandon it. The ear … The nose … The tongue … The body … The mind is not yours — abandon it. Phenomena are not yours — abandon them. Mind-consciousness is not yours — abandon it. Mind-contact is not yours — abandon it. Whatever feeling arises dependent on mind-contact — pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant — that feeling is not yours. Abandon it. Abandoning it will lead to your happiness and well-being.”

title: “What are the five mental shackles not yet cut off? Monks, here a monk is not free from lust, desire, affection, thirst, fever, craving for…
content: “What are the five mental shackles not yet cut off? Monks, here a monk is not free from lust, desire, affection, thirst, fever, craving for sensual pleasures. Monks, for a monk who is not free from lust … craving for sensual pleasures, his mind does not incline to exertion, commitment, persistence, striving. If one’s mind does not incline to exertion, commitment, persistence, striving, this is the first mental shackle not yet cut off.”
